description:

Our group’s chosen island from Atlas of Remote Islands is the Banaba Island. We were intrigued by the unique lifestyle and rituals that were carried out there and wanted to build our concept on it. On the Banaba Island, the people would tattoo themselves all over their body as a preparation for the afterlife. As they journey towards the west, Nei Karamakuna, a woman with a bird head, would feed on their tattoos and grant spirit eyes to the dead as thanks. However, if one were to lack tattoos, she would peck out their eyes and condemn them to blindness forever. While bird is a form of deity and god-like on the island, the people also dance around tame birds and throw objects at them, and when they cannot move, they would flatten their wings till they’re dead.

Initially we were very interested in using the tattoos as the main visual focus for our work and researched into different types of tribal tattoos that exist and compiled them together. We were moving from the perspective of the islanders and wanted to making an audio-visual interactive experience through the mapping of tattoos on our audiences’ skin incorporated with natural wildlife-like background audio. However after consultation in class, we decided to alter the perspective of our audience in the narration to be the divine being from the Banaba island instead.

Our project consists of three aspects, we generated a strudel audio for the background, created the visuals of the tribal tattoos, as well as the midi controller for input and interaction. We decided to incorporate both day and night styles with the day version consisting of the capturing of audiences’ skintone through a webcam instead of just fixing a singular shade from our end. Using the keys on the midipad, the audience can perform an action similar to the bird divine, erasing the tattoos off the screen. The keys also have a randomised sound mapped onto them, like the splashing of water or the thud of an object landing, mimicking the natural sounds that can be heard on an island by sea and the sound of rocks hitting a surface. We wanted to use a diversity of sounds and audios to create a spatial experience, mirroring the Banaba island’s life.
